Introduction
Myopathies are a diverse group of diseases characterized by weak-
ness caused by dysfunction of muscle fibers. The most common 
symptoms are weakness, cramps, rhabdomyolysis, and exercise  
intolerance. The pathophysiology of myopathies involves disrup -
tion of the muscle tissue integrity or internal metabolic processes, 
which can occur by either a hereditary or an acquired process. 
Hereditary myopathies can present in childhood, adolescence, or 
adulthood. Genetic mutations lead to deficiencies of the muscle 
architecture (muscular dystrophies), energy storage/processing 
(metabolic myopathies), or electrolyte channels (channelopathies). 
Acquired myopathies can be caused by a variety of causes, includ-
ing autoimmune, toxic, endocrine, infectious, or medication  
related. Although the differential is broad, accurate diagnosis is 
important because many of these disorders have disease-modifying 
treatments.
Most myopathic disorders show a symmetric, proximal pattern 
of weakness, although there are key exceptions where weakness  
is asymmetric and preferentially affects certain muscle groups. 
Certain myopathic disorders cause dysfunction of other organ 
systems. Weakness of the diaphragm and respiratory muscles can 
cause restrictive lung disease, and weakness of the oropharyngeal 
muscles result in dysarthria and dysphagia. Cardiomyopathy and 
conduction abnormalities are found in many hereditary muscular 
dystrophies. The ideal treatment of these patients combines 
medical therapies, multidisciplinary care, and rehabilitation man-
agement. The physiatrist is uniquely equipped to provide compre-
hensive care for this population.
History
A comprehensive history is essential for timely diagnosis and 
selecting the appropriate diagnostic tests. Myopathies typically 
involve motor impairment without sensory symptoms.
Determining the age of onset and rate of progression are cru -
cial when differentiating between genetic and acquired myopa -
thies. Most genetic myopathic disorders are present in childhood, 
adolescence, or by the third decade of life, whereas acquired  
myopathies can present at any age. Acquired myopathies usually 
present more rapidly with progressive weakness over a period of 
weeks or months, whereas hereditary myopathies can have a more 
indolent course over a period of years or decades. Episodic bouts 
of weakness with normal strength in between points toward a 
metabolic myopathy or periodic paralysis.23 A birth and develop-
mental history, including motor and cognitive developmental 
milestones, should be assessed. Hypotonia at birth may indicate a 
myopathic process, but spinal muscular atrophy and hereditary 
neuropathies are also considerations. For adult patients, the pro -
vider should also ask whether the patient had any issues with 
• Neuromuscular weakness leading to restrictive lung disease is 
one of the most serious manifestations in myopathic disorders, 
and management typically requires collaboration with a respi -
ratory therapist and a pulmonologist.
• Strength and mobility assessments should be conducted at 
each visit for patients to evaluate the need for mobility aids  
and adaptive equipment.
• Pain is common in individuals with muscle diseases due a 
variety of factors, including musculoskeletal dysfunction, 
altered biomechanics, and joint contractures, requiring 
comprehensive evaluation and treatment strategies,  
including medications, therapies, imaging, and procedural 
interventions.
43
Myopathic Disorders
HAIBI (DANIEL) CAI AND SARAH SMITH
• Myopathies are a diverse group of diseases with symptoms of 
weakness, cramps, and exercise intolerance. The two main cate-
gories are hereditary myopathies and acquired myopathies.
• Symmetric proximal weakness without sensory loss is the most 
common presentation. Specific myopathies such as facioscapu-
lohumeral muscular dystrophy or inclusion body myositis have 
asymmetric involvement of select muscle groups.
• Diagnostic testing for myopathic diseases includes electromy-
ography and nerve conduction studies, ultrasonography and/or 
magnetic resonance imaging, muscle biopsy, laboratory testing, 
exercise testing, and genetic testing.
• Causes of acquired muscle diseases are highly variable and may 
include autoimmune, endocrine, toxic, and infectious etiologies.

physical activity and keeping up with peers in their youth, par -
ticularly in strength and endurance events. Muscle cramping and 
pain are often present. A history of rhabdomyolysis may also be 
present, and the provider should ask if there have been any epi -
sodes of dark red (cola color) urine.
Recognizing the pattern of weakness is essential. The majority 
of myopathic disorders, both acquired and hereditary, showed a 
proximal pattern of weakness preferentially affecting the shoulder 
muscles and pelvic girdle. This pattern holds true for the most 
common genetic myopathies, such as Duchenne muscular dystro-
phy (DMD) and limb girdle muscular dystrophy (LGMD). Most 
acquired myopathies, such as dermatomyositis, statin-induced 
myopathy, and steroid myopathy, also have this proximal pattern 
of weakness. Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy (FSHD) 
presents with asymmetric weakness in the face, scapula, elbow 
flexors, elbow extensors, hip flexors, and dorsiflexors. Inclusion 
body myositis (IBM) is the most common myositis in adults over 
age 65 years and characteristically affects the long finger flexors 
and knee extensors.
A broad review of systems needs to be conducted for extramus-
cular symptoms. Respiratory muscle weakness can present with 
dyspnea on exertion and difficulty breathing when lying flat  
(orthopnea). Late-onset Pompe disease has significant diaphragm 
weakness, and this may be the presenting symptom. A cardiac 
evaluation for chest pains, palpitations, and swelling should be 
conducted. Cardiomyopathy and cardiac conduction abnormali -
ties are frequently found in patients with myotonic muscular 
dystrophy, DMD, Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy (EDMD), 
and select LGMD. The patient should be assessed for any difficul-
ties with speech, swallowing, or saliva management. Dysphagia 
can be seen in IBM and oculopharyngeal muscular dystrophy 
(OPMD).23
Exercise intolerance and fatigue are associated with metabolic 
myopathies, although these may be present in other myopathies 
as well. The type of exercise intolerance can be critical in deter -
mining the diagnosis. For example, glycogen storage disorders, 
such as McArdle disease, are characterized by muscle stiffness and 
weakness immediately after high-intensity exercise. Conversely, 
fatty acid oxidation defects, such as carnitine palmitoyltransferase 
II (CPT-II) deficiency, show exercise intolerance to low-intensity, 
long-endurance exercises.
The past medical history and medication history should be 
carefully reviewed. A history of autoimmune diseases, cancer,  
or skin manifestations can point toward inflammatory myopa -
thy. Hyperthyroidism or calcium deficiency can also lead to 
myopathy. The most common medication-induced muscle dis -
orders include statins, steroids, and colchicine. A detailed listing  
of inherited and acquired myopathic conditions is located in 
Table 43.1.
Family History
Many myopathic disorders are hereditary in nature, and a detailed 
family history is necessary. Not all patients or parents can identify 
if a family member has a history of “muscular dystrophy.” Asking 
if patients have any family members who have an unusual gait, use 
braces or a wheelchair, or have a history of cardiac issues from a 
young age can be helpful. Completing a family pedigree may also 
provide insight into transmission of disease. Duchenne and Becker 
muscular dystrophies are X-linked recessive disorders that affect 
only males from the maternal side. Autosomal dominant disorders, 
such as FSHD, have a 50% chance of affecting the offspring,  
regardless of sex, and many family members can be affected. In 
autosomal recessive disorders, such as LGMD, both parents are 
usually unaffected carriers, and the offspring have a 25% chance of 
getting the disease. In these cases, there may be no other family 
members involved or it may be a sibling that is affected.
Physical Exam
A comprehensive examination is essential to diagnosis. Manual 
muscle testing should be completed bilaterally in key muscle 
groups proximally and distally. Proximal weakness preferentially 
affecting shoulder abduction, external rotation, and internal rota-
tion as well as hip flexion, hip abduction, and hip adduction is the 
most common pattern of weakness in myopathies. This is the 
pattern seen in DMD, Becker muscular dystrophy (BMD), and 
LGMD, as well as inflammatory myositis, statin-induced myopa-
thy, steroid myopathy, and critical illness myopathy (CIM). Selec-
tive weakness of the finger flexors and knee extensors is seen in 
IBM. When testing finger flexors, have the patient place the hand 
supine and stabilize the distal interphalangeal joint to isolate the 
long finger flexors. Distal predominant weakness is rarer and can 
be seen in dysferlinopathy (LGMD type 2B), myofibrillar myopa-
thies, and other late adult-onset muscular dystrophies.
Inspection of muscle bulk and appearance can be helpful. Atro-
phy of select muscle groups can point toward a certain diagnosis. 
Scapular winging is seen in FSHD, LGMD-2A, and LGMD-2C,D 
(Fig. 43.1 ). Pseudohypertrophy of the calves is seen in DMD  
(Fig. 43.2). EDMD and Ullrich congenital myopathy characteris-
tically have elbow flexion contractures. Many of the congenital 
myopathies and congenital muscular dystrophies result in abnor -
mal development in utero, leading to arthrogryposis (contractures) 
at birth.
Facial weakness can be seen in myotonic muscular dystrophy 
and FSHD. Weakness in ocular muscles or ptosis can be seen in 
FSMD and OPMD (Fig. 43.3). Macroglossia (enlargement of the 
tongue) can be seen in Pompe disease and amyloidosis. A high-
arched palate or dental malocclusion can be seen in congenital 
myopathies and congenital muscular dystrophies. Patients with 
myotonic muscular dystrophy type I often have a characteristic 
hatchet-face appearance, tenting of the upper lip, temporal wast -
ing, and frontal balding (Fig. 43.4).
Sensory examination should be intact in most myopathies. 
Reflexes are either normal or diminished based on the amount of 
weakness. Myotonic muscular dystrophies and channelopathies 
may present with grip myotonia. This can be assessed by having 
the patient maximally close both fists for 10 seconds and then 
relax them. Grip myotonia is observed if there is delayed relax -
ation. Percussion myotonia involves tapping the thenar eminence 
with a reflex hammer and assessing for involuntary contraction 
(Fig. 43.5).
If appropriate, the patient’s gait and functional movements 
should be also evaluated. A “myopathic gait” is characterized by 
waddling of the hips from side to side, lumbar lordosis, and 
sometimes toe walking. Patients compensate for hip extension 
weakness by arching the lumbar spine posteriorly (lordosis) to 
keep the center of gravity over the pelvis. Similarly, they com -
pensate for hip abduction weakness by leaning the trunk of the 
body over the standing leg during swing phase, which is also 
known as a compensated T rendelenburg gait (Video 43.1). Core 
and proximal hip strength can be further tested by asking the 
patient to get up out of a chair with the arms crossed or to per -
form squats.

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
Diagnostic Evaluation
Laboratory Testing
Laboratory testing should be guided by the clinical and family 
history as well as the patient’s physical examination. Creatine  
kinase (CK) is an enzyme found in various body tissues, including 
muscle, and it is one of the most useful labs to obtain in a  
myopathy workup. CK levels become elevated in muscle disease 
as it leaks out from damaged muscle cells into the bloodstream. 
The CK level therefore often correlates to the degree of muscle 
damage. CK levels may be elevated in both hereditary and  
acquired forms of muscle disease secondary to a variety of under -
lying mechanisms, such as metabolic defects, genetic abnormali -
ties, damage from toxins, and inflammation. CK levels can even  
become elevated in individuals without muscle disease after 
strenuous exercise. Elevated CK levels are therefore a nonspecific 
finding. Aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotrans-
ferase (ALT), aldolase, and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) may also 
be elevated in muscle disease and are often ordered as part of the 
laboratory evaluation. However, AST , ALT , LDH, and aldolase 
may all be elevated in hepatic dysfunction as well. A gamma-
glutamyl transferase level, which is specific for liver disease, 
should therefore also be obtained to differentiate underlying  
pathology. Based on the patient’s history, other useful labs may 
include an electrolyte panel to evaluate potassium and calcium 
levels, thyroid function tests, inflammatory markers (including 
erythrocyte sedimentation rate), rheumatologic labs (such as anti-
nuclear antibodies), and serum immunofixation, which may be 
abnormal in acquired amyloidosis. Specialized panels to look for 
autoantibodies associated with inflammatory myopathies, such as 
hydroxymethylglutaryl–coenzyme A (HMG-CoA) reductase anti-
bodies, may also be considered when the history is suggestive.  
In circumstances where a neuromuscular junction disorder such 
• Fig. 43.5 Percussion myotonia whereby a localized myotonic contrac -
tion results from percussion over the abductor pollicis brevis muscle.
as myasthenia gravis is suspected, specific autoantibodies, such  
as acetylcholine receptor antibodies (among others), should be 
obtained. Diagnostic tests for select myopathic disorders can be 
found in Table 43.2.
Electrodiagnostic Testing
Nerve Conduction Studies
Electrodiagnostic testing is key in differentiating neuropathic eti -
ologies from myopathic etiologies in the evaluation of weakness. 
Sensory nerve conduction studies are usually normal in myopa -
thies, though certain myopathic conditions, such as IBM, can be 
associated with a polyneuropathy.
16 Motor nerve conduction stud-
ies are also usually normal, though compound motor action poten-
tial amplitudes can be low in particularly severe or end-stage  
myopathies affecting distal muscles. Significant delays in onset  
latencies, reduced conduction velocities, conduction blocks, and 
temporal dispersion are not characteristic of myopathies and should 
alert the electromyographer to the possibility of a neuropathy. If a 
neuromuscular junction disorder is on the differential, techniques 
such as repetitive nerve stimulation may also be employed to look 
for evidence of a defect in neuromuscular transmission.
Electromyography
Needle electromyography (EMG) can provide several diagnostic 
clues through the evaluation of abnormal spontaneous activity  
in addition to motor unit action potential analysis. Fibrillation 
potentials, positive sharp waves, and complex repetitive discharges 
are common in neuropathic conditions; however, these waveforms 
can also be seen in certain myopathic etiologies, especially inflam-
matory myopathies. Myotonic discharges are most commonly seen 
in myotonic dystrophies, though may also be appreciated in toxic, 
hereditary, and metabolic myopathies. A unique electrodiagnostic 
feature in metabolic myopathies, such as McCardle disease, is a 
contracture, which is defined by the perplexing absence of any 
EMG activity during a muscle contraction.
Motor units in myopathic conditions are classically character -
ized by low amplitude, short duration, and increased polyphasia. 
Changes in motor unit morphology are secondary to loss of indi-
vidual muscle fibers. Early recruitment is another common fea -
ture observed in motor unit analysis, defined by an abnormally 
high volume of motor units for the degree of patient effort. It 
must be emphasized that the recruitment ratio is normal in early 
recruitment, and there are simply more motor units than expected 
for a minimal degree of muscle contraction ( Video 43.2).
It should be noted that certain myopathies, such as IBM, may 
demonstrate a mixture of motor unit morphology and recruitment. 
Both long-duration, high-amplitude motor units with reduced  
recruitment and short-duration, low-amplitude motor units with 
early recruitment may be observed within the same muscle in some 
patients with IBM.
16 Of note, steroid myopathy often demon -
strates no abnormalities on EMG. Abnormal spontaneous activity 
is typically not seen in steroid myopathy. Additionally, motor unit 
analysis may be normal as steroid myopathy predominantly affects 
type II muscle fibers and EMG assesses mostly type I muscle fibers.
Imaging
Magnetic Resonance Imaging
Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can be a useful tool across the 
spectrum of muscle diseases. It can detect characteristic patterns 
of muscle involvement, assess disease severity, and help select the

most appropriate biopsy site by identifying areas of active inflam-
mation, necrosis, or abnormal muscle tissue. 1,29,40 Additionally, 
MRI is increasingly being used as an outcome measure in clinical 
trials.44 Quantitative MRI techniques, such as muscle volumetry, 
diffusion tensor imaging, and magnetic resonance spectroscopy, 
provide objective measures of muscle structure, function, and 
metabolism, which can be used to assess treatment efficacy and 
monitor disease progression.
Ultrasound
Some of the earliest research performed using ultrasound in the 
field of neuromuscular medicine was driven by Heckmatt in the 
1980s in the study of muscle disease. Heckmatt et al. described  
the now famous Heckmatt scale,
20 a 4-point scale grading the sever-
ity of muscle disease based on the echogenicity. There are clear  
advantages in using ultrasound over MRI, namely how easily and 
affordably it can be performed at the bedside. Ultrasound can pro-
vide similar benefits to MRI in evaluating muscle disease, including 
identifying patterns of abnormalities characteristic of specific  
diseases, assisting in muscle biopsy site selection, and assessing dis-
ease severity. Ultrasound can be especially helpful in myopathies 
with selective muscle involvement, such as IBM, where the flexor 
digitorum profundus shows increased hyperechogenicity and loss  
of muscle echotexture when compared to the flexor carpi ulnaris 
(Fig. 43.6). Ultrasound is less sensitive than MRI in detecting 
muscle edema, though it can provide similar information about 
tissue architecture and the presence of pathology.
53
Muscle Biopsy
Though less commonly performed in recent decades, muscle biopsy 
can still provide critical information in certain cases leading to the 
correct diagnosis. Muscle biopsy can also give a sense of disease  
severity at times. Muscle biopsy selection site is guided by the clues 
from the history and examination, surgical accessibility, likelihood 
of diagnostic yield, and goal of minimizing harm from the proce-
dure. As mentioned earlier in this chapter, imaging with MRI or 
ultrasound can help identify an appropriate muscle for biopsy. The 
goal is to obtain a sample from a muscle that clearly demonstrates 
pathology; however, the muscle selected should not be so advanced 
in the disease course that it is end stage because severe atrophy and 
fibrosis can impair analysis. A Medical Research Council strength

12 Muscles that have been recently studied with 
needle EMG should be avoided.
The procedure itself is typically performed under local anes -
thesia. The type of surgeon who performs the procedure will vary 
by institution, but it is often a general surgeon, neurosurgeon, 
orthopedic surgeon, or plastic surgeon. In some institutions, 
nonsurgical physicians, such as neurologists, have privileges to 
perform the procedure. The procedure can be done either as an 
open biopsy or needle biopsy. An open biopsy has the advantage 
of providing a larger specimen for pathologic analysis and is  
often preferred for that reason.
36 The biopsy specimen is sent to 
pathology for further evaluation. Frozen sections are then pro -
cessed with various techniques, the choice of which depends  
on the clinical context and laboratory performing the analysis.  
A common protocol may involve hematoxylin and eosin stain -
ing, histochemistry for Gömöri trichrome, enzyme histochemis -
try for nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide tetrazolium reductase, 
succinate dehydrogenase, cytochrome C oxidase/succinate dehy -
drogenase combination, and fiber typing using slow myosin and 
fast myosin immunohistochemistry. These studies can help dif -
ferentiate neurogenic from myogenic pathology and can identify 
common features of inflammatory myopathies, IBM, congenital/
inherited myopathies, vacuolar myopathies, metabolic myopa -
thies, vasculitis, and amyloidosis.
36
Forearm Exercise Testing
Forearm exercise testing can be used in the evaluation of meta -
bolic myopathies. A nonischemic forearm exercise test is per -
formed by taking a serum lactate and ammonia level from the 
antecubital vein before and after effortful hand grip dynamometry 
protocol.
48 A significant increase in ammonia levels without an 
increase in lactate is characteristic of glycogen storage diseases 
(GSD). Of note, nonischemic forearm exercise testing is preferred 
over ischemic forearm exercise testing because of the risk of rhab-
domyolysis and compartment syndrome with the latter.
Genetic Testing
Advances in genetic testing have provided incredible improve-
ments in diagnostics in recent years. The expense of genetic testing 
and requirement of prior authorization from insurance can still be 
a barrier in some cases, though sponsored testing has become more 
common for certain diseases and incurs no cost to the patient. 
Patients who have lived with undifferentiated muscle diseases for 
decades may now have access to testing that can identify a specific 
etiology. In some cases, genetic testing is all that is needed to con-
firm a diagnosis, and patients can avoid undergoing uncomfortable 
procedures, such as electrodiagnostic testing and muscle biopsy. 
Genetic testing can also provide important information about 
disease manifestations affecting other organ systems. For example, 
knowing that certain genetic conditions are also associated with 
cardiac disease will allow the provider to perform appropriate 
screening and refer to specialists. Knowing the genetic etiology can 
also provide important information regarding the patient’s progno-
sis. Patients can also use this information to seek genetic counsel-
ing for family planning. Finally, understanding the genetics of 
specific muscle diseases has profound treatment implications. 
There are now several US Food and Drug Administration (FDA)–
approved gene therapies available for patients with identified  
mutations (see later).

Hereditary Myopathies
Muscular Dystrophies
Muscular dystrophies are a group of hereditary disorders charac -
terized by progressive muscle weakness and dystrophic muscle  
fibers with fatty replacement on biopsy. Some muscular dystro-
phies are associated with cardiac or respiratory dysfunction or 
other organ involvement.
31 Various mutations causing disruption 
in muscle structure or function are involved in muscular dystro-
phies (Fig. 43.7).
Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy and Becker Muscular  
Dystrophy. DMD and BMD are X-linked recessive diseases 
caused by mutations in the dystrophin gene. Dystrophin is a  
cytoskeletal protein, which promotes stability and functionality  
of muscle fibers. The gene encoding dystrophin is one of the larg-
est coding genes in the human genome, and it is thus prone to 
mutation. DMD affects approximately 15 cases per 100,000 live 
male births, whereas BMD is less common. DMD patients have 
a more severe phenotype. In DMD, the dystrophin gene makes a 
nonfunctional protein cause by either a frameshift mutation or 
nonsense mutation creating a premature stop codon.
4 In BMD, 
however, there is usually a missense mutation preserving the natu-
ral open reading frame, resulting in a truncated protein that is still 
somewhat functional.
Disease progression in DMD is well characterized. Initial signs 
begin in early childhood (ages 2 to 6 years) with weakness, clumsi-
ness, difficulty climbing stairs, requiring use of hands to get up 
from the floor (Gower sign) (Fig. 43.8), and hypertrophied calves.
4 
Difficulties with ambulation progress throughout childhood, and 
the median age for loss of ambulation is 13 to 14 years. After tran-
sitioning to a wheelchair for mobility, patients continue to have 
progressive weakness of the upper extremities and worsening car -
diomyopathy and restrictive lung disease. Most patients die from 
cardiac or pulmonary complications before the fourth decade  
of life.
5
Diagnosis. Patients with DMD have significantly elevated CK 
and aldolase levels. Genetic testing is the primary method of di -
agnosis, and current methods can detect a mutation in dystrophin 
in most cases. In the rare cases where genetic testing is not unre -
vealing, muscle biopsy shows muscle fiber size variation, fibrosis, 
and abnormal staining of dystrophin.
4
Family members of a patient with DMD should consider ge -
netic testing. Female carriers have several reproductive choices, 
including preimplantation genetic diagnosis or prenatal genetic 
testing. Female carriers are at increased risk of cardiomyopathy 
and should receive regular screening.
Disease-Modifying Therapies.  Although there is no cure for 
DMD, there are many existing and emerging treatment options 
focused on slowing disease progression and improving quality of 
life. DMD is the most researched hereditary muscular dystrophy 
in terms of active clinical trials and pharmaceutic pipeline. In the 
past few years, there have been multiple medications approved for 
DMD that target the upstream genetic dysfunction in dystrophin 
and the downstream biochemical pathways responsible for muscle 
degeneration.
Corticosteroids have been used as disease-modifying treatment 
for DMD for over 30 years and have been shown to slow progres-
sion of weakness, delay loss of ambulation, and preserve upper

extremity strength. Many DMD patients are started on steroids in 
early childhood. Glucocorticoids have a significant side effect 
profile, including hyperglycemia, cataracts, weight gain, osteopo -
rosis, and delayed puberty.
47 Novel formulations of steroids, such 
as deflazacort and vamorolone, have shown similar efficacy in 
terms of preserving muscle strength and prolonging ambulation 
while demonstrating a better side effect profile compared to tradi-
tional steroids.
17,18 The efficacy of continuing steroids in the late 
nonambulatory stage is an area requiring further research.
Exon skipping therapies are a novel class of medications that 
use antisense oligonucleotides to “skip” over mutated exons of the 
dystrophin gene, theoretically restoring the open reading frame 
and creating a functional, albeit truncated, protein. These treat -
ments are only available to patients with specific mutations in 
dystrophin that are amenable to skipping. Eteplirsen (exon 51) 
and golodirsen (exon 53) are FDA-approved intravenous treat -
ments available to DMD patients with these specific mutations 
and have shown relative increases in dystrophin levels in clinical 
trials.
15,27
In 2023, the FDA approved Elevidys (delandistrogene moxepar-
vovec) as the first gene transfer therapy for DMD patients between 
the ages of 4 and 5 years. Elevidys uses an adeno-associated virus 
vector to deliver a shortened version of the dystrophin gene to skel-
etal and cardiac muscles resulting in production of a truncated 
dystrophin protein (microdystrophin). This is a one-time intrave-
nous medication. Results from the clinical trials show improvement 
in functional rating scales, time to rise, and 10-m walk test.
41,55
Duvyzat (givinostat) is a histone deacetylase inhibitor indi -
cated for individuals with DMD age 6 years and older. Absence 
of dystrophin results in decreased expression of genes involved in 
muscle regeneration through histone deacetylase activity.
32 In  
a clinical trial, DMD patients on Duvyzat were shown to have  
improved four-stair climb assessment compared to placebo.  
Duvyzat received FDA approval in 2024.
Rehabilitation. DMD has a well-characterized pattern of pro -
gression with worsening weakness throughout childhood, loss of 
ambulation and transition to wheelchair use in teenage years, and 
ultimately cardiac and pulmonary dysfunction leading to mortality. 
Rehabilitation management uses our knowledge of natural history 
to develop specific interventions to improve function and quality  
of life at each stage. A multidisciplinary care team, including a 
physician, physical therapist, occupational therapist, speech ther-
apy, orthotist, and an equipment specialist, is recommended.
Ambulatory Stage. Patients benefit from therapies to improve 
gait, minimize risk of falls, and assess for appropriate assistive 
devices. Patients should undergo a daily home stretching program 
to prevent contractures at the ankles, knees, and hips early on, 
while adding in stretches for the upper extremities later. Resting 
ankle-foot orthosis can be used to prevent plantarflexion contrac-
tures. Education regarding exercise in muscular dystrophy should 
be provided, emphasizing aerobic exercise and avoiding eccentric 
high-resistance exercises and monitoring for overexertion.
Nonambulatory Stage.  T ransitioning to a wheelchair can be  
a physically and emotionally challenging process. Most DMD 
patients benefit from a motorized power wheelchair because of 
their inability to propel a manual wheelchair. Custom-molded 
seating can be helpful for axial support and offloading pressure 
over the sacrum and ischial tuberosities. Power tilt and recline 
reduce the risk of pressure injuries and help relieve stress over the 
lower back and hips. A pressure-relieving cushion should be used. 
Wrist/hand orthosis and knee orthotics, or serial casting, can be 
used to prevent contractures. Dynamic arm support splints can be 
helpful for proximal weakness and improve independence in  
activities of daily living (ADLs).
Cardiac. Individuals with DMD develop cardiomyopathy from 
dysfunction of dystrophin in cardiac muscle fibers. Involving a 
cardiologist early in the disease course is recommended. After age 
10 years, DMD patients should have cardiac screening yearly with 
electrocardiogram (ECG) and cardiac imaging. Cardiovascular 
MRI is superior to echocardiogram in assessing cardiomyopathy, 
left ventricular dysfunction, and muscle fibrosis. DMD patients are 
at increased risk for rhythm abnormalities and should undergo 
regular Holter motoring. Angiotensin-converting enzyme or angio-
tensin receptor blockers are first-line therapy with the addition of 
beta blockers and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists later in 
the disease course.
5 Heart transplantation has been shown to be a 
viable option for end-stage heart disease in slower progressing mus-
cular dystrophies such as BMD.51 However, cardiac transplantation 
in DMD is a complex ethical dilemma because late-stage DMD 
patients also suffer from significant restrictive lung disease, gastro-
intestinal dysfunction, and limited functional independence, which 
often excludes these patients from transplant lists.

Pulmonary. DMD patients develop progressive restrictive lung 
disease from diaphragm and respiratory muscle weakness and 
chest wall deformities. Loss of ambulation is associated with  
decreasing forced vital capacity and onset of pulmonary dysfunc -
tion. Early symptoms of respiratory dysfunction include impaired 
cough reflex, nocturnal hypoventilation, and frequent respiratory 
infections, while later symptoms include frank dyspnea and  
orthopnea. Routine pulmonary function testing (PFTs) should be 
assessed measuring functional vital capacity, maximum inspira -
tory pressure, and maximum expiratory pressure. Nocturnal  
hypoventilation can be assessed by asking questions about quality 
of sleep, fatigue, impaired concentration, nighttime arousals, 
morning headaches, and the need to take naps throughout the 
day. Patients with symptoms of nocturnal hypoventilation should 
be assessed with polysomnography. A weak cough reflex can lead 
to mucous plugging, atelectasis, or pneumonia. A mechanical  
insufflation-exsufflation (cough-assist) device gradually inflates 
the lungs and then rapidly changes to negative pressure to clear 
secretions. Initiation of noninvasive ventilation (NIV) at night 
has been shown to improve survival and improve nocturnal  
hypoventilation. In late-stage disease, as respiratory function  
decreases, patients will gradually require NIV throughout the day 
as well.5 In the past, patients with muscular dystrophy and restric-
tive lung disease often received tracheostomies, especially when 
they were hospitalized in the setting of acute respiratory failure. 
However, improvements in our understanding of noninvasive 
pulmonary care, especially the addition of daytime mouthpiece 
ventilation, has drastically reduced the need for tracheostomies. 
Most DMD patients with end-stage pulmonary dysfunction can 
maintain stable vital capacity and normocapnia with NIV . 50  
T racheostomy can still be considered in some cases, and consider-
ations would include a discussion regarding possible impairments 
to speech, swallowing, and the need for increased respiratory care.
Gastrointestinal and Nutritional Management.  Patients with 
DMD can have dysphagia, abnormal weight, and nutritional 
imbalances caused by immobility, decreased energy expenditure, 
and glucocorticoid therapy. DMD patients should regularly be 
assessed by a dietician to track intake, nutritional status, and 
weight. Proper nutritional care is essential to prevent either obe -
sity or malnourishment. Dysphagia typically presents late in 
DMD. Mild dysphagia can be treated with speech therapy evalu -
ation for swallowing techniques and diet modification. Severe 
dysphagia requires placement of a gastrostomy tube.
Endocrine Complications.  DMD and the use of corticoste -
roids lead to endocrine complications of impaired growth,  
delayed puberty, and adrenal insufficiency.
Growth and Puberty. Linear growth should be assessed every  
6 months until completion of puberty. For nonstanding patients, 
height can be approximated segmentally measuring the individual 
body parts. Patients with a significant decline in growth trajectory 
or a height less than the third percentile should be referred to 
endocrinology.
Delayed puberty is a potential complication of glucocorticoid 
therapy. Patients should be regularly checked for pubertal devel -
opment, and biochemical testing should be performed for con -
cern for delayed puberty. T estosterone replacement therapy is 
recommended for confirmed hypogonadism in DMD patients 
older than 14 years.4
Adrenal Insufficiency.  Adrenal crisis caused by chronic sup -
pression of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis is a potentially 
life-threatening condition that can develop if glucocorticoids  
are stopped suddenly. Signs of adrenal crisis include lethargy,  
abdominal pain, worsening weakness, decreased appetite, dizzi -
ness, fever, dehydration, diarrhea, or hypoglycemia. Stress dosing 
of steroids may be required for patients with severe illness, major 
trauma, or surgery. Glucocorticosteroids cannot be stopped 
abruptly but can be very gradually tapered over a period of 
months via the PJ Nicholoff Steroid Protocol.25
Bone Health.  T reatment with glucocorticoids can lead to  
osteoporosis, putting DMD patients at higher risk for long bone 
and vertebral compression fractures. Laboratory testing for cal -
cium, vitamin D, phosphate, magnesium, and parathyroid hor -
mone should be assessed regularly. Dual x-ray absorptiometry 
scan should be performed to assess bone mineral density; how -
ever, compared to healthy individuals, bone mineral density may 
not be sufficient in evaluating for osteoporosis. Vertebral fractures 
in DMD patients can often be asymptomatic, and regular thora -
columbar x-rays are recommended even if the patient does not 
have low back pain. Dietary supplements with calcium and vita -
min D are recommended. In patients with severe osteoporosis or 
a history of long bone or vertebral fractures, treatment with 
bisphosphonates should be considered.4
Orthopedic Complications.  In the ambulatory stage, plan -
tarflexion contractures that are negatively impacting ambulation 
can be considered for Achilles tendon–lengthening surgery.  
Assessment for scoliosis should be completed at least annually via 
physical assessment and spinal radiographs. Worsening of scoliosis 
occurs often after loss of ambulation. Posterior spinal fusion 
should be considered for spinal curve greater than 20 degrees, 
especially in patients who are nonambulatory or have not reached 
puberty.4 In the late nonambulatory stage, surgical interventions 
for contractures are not recommended unless there are issues with 
pain, positioning, or skin integrity.
Limb Girdle Muscular Dystrophy. LGMDs are a broad group 
of hereditary disorders involving skeletal muscles, which are char-
acterized by symmetric proximal weakness of shoulder and pelvic 
girdles. LGMDs have an estimated incidence of 1 to 6 of 100,000, 
although this figure may be an underestimate given the variability 
of presentation and underdiagnosis of these disorders.22 Based on 
the gene involved, clinical symptoms may present at any time 
from early childhood to late adulthood. Initial weakness is typi -
cally in proximal muscles of the arms and legs, although with 
progression, weakness may spread to more distal muscle groups 
and/or axial and neck muscles. Some types of LGMD are also 
associated with cardiac or pulmonary dysfunction. Extraocular 
muscles and cranial muscles are usually sparred.
The increased availability and affordability of molecular  
genetic testing have drastically changed the diagnostic algorithm 
in LGMDs. Multigene panels can test large numbers of genes  
simultaneously. Often genetic testing alone can lead to swift and 
accurate diagnosis. However, genetic testing can result in variants 
of unknown significance (VUS), in which there is a mutation in 
a causative gene; however, it is uncertain whether this mutation 
sufficiently disrupts protein function. In these cases, further inves-
tigations are required. Muscle biopsy shows dystrophic changes 
with muscle fiber necrosis and regeneration and replacement with 
fibrosis and fatty tissue. Immunohistologic staining can be help -
ful. In most LGMD, CK is elevated. EMG of weak muscles 
should show myopathic motor units.
Some forms of LGMD are inherited in an autosomal dominant 
pattern, while others are recessive. Recently, there has been consid-
erable debate into the classifications of LGMDs and which diseases 
qualify as LGMDs. The original classification system for LGMDs 
was derived in the 1995 by the European Neuromuscular Centre,

2018, the same group proposed a new nomenclature of using

genotypes that were thought to not fit the phenotypical description 
of LGMD were excluded. For ease of understanding, both nomen-
clatures are shown in our discussion, and the most common types 
of LGMDs are described. Next is a list of the most common  
LGMDs. A comprehensive list of the many mutations resulting in 
this phenotype is beyond the scope of this chapter.
LGMD-R1/LGMD-2A Calpainopathy. Calpainopathy is the 
most common form of LGMD worldwide. Age of onset is  
between 2 and 45 years. The limb girdle pattern of weakness is 
most common. Scapular winging is common in this disorder, and 
calpainopathy should be in the differential diagnosis for suspected 
FSHD. Joint contractures are found in more than one-half of 
patients, frequently affecting the ankles, hips, knees, and elbows. 
The heart and lungs are usually not affected.52
LGMD-R2/LGMD-2B Dysferlinopathy.  Dysferlinopathy is 
the second most common subtype of LGMD in the United 
States; however, in Asia, it is the most common subtype. Age of 
onset is usually between 15 and 30 years. Dysferlinopathy can 
present with the typical proximal pattern of weakness, but it also 
has a predilection for distal lower extremity weakness, particularly 
in plantarflexion. Calves are usually atrophied. Interestingly,  
before onset of symptoms, patients with dysferlinopathies tend to 
be more athletic than their peers, with roughly 20% competing in 
sports on a regional or national level. Cardiac involvement is rare. 
Pulmonary complications may arise late in disease course. CK can 
be markedly elevated (.30,000 U/L).52
LGMD-2C(D,E,F)/LGMD-R3(4,5,6) Sarcoglycanopathy. The 
sarcoglycan proteins (alpha-, beta-, gamma-, and delta-sarcoglycan) 
form a tetrameric structure in the muscle membrane. Mutation in 
any of the four sarcoglycan proteins can result in myopathy, and the 
phenotypes of the four sarcoglycanopathies are similar. Disease 
onset is usually within the first decade of life, beginning with a 
typical pattern of proximal upper and lower extremity weakness. 
Patients often have calf hypertrophy, macroglossia, scapular wing-
ing, and scoliosis. Weakness can be severe, and the majority of  
patients are wheelchair dependent by the time they are teenagers. 
Dilated cardiomyopathy and restrictive lung disease are common, 
and patients should have regular screening.
Myofibrillar Myopathy/Previously LGMD-1A.  This disease 
process involves mutations in the gene myotilin and can cause 
progressive weakness both proximally and distally. Previously clas-
sified as LGMD-1A, recent proposals suggest removing this dis -
ease from LGMD because of its predilection for distal weakness. 
The age of onset has a wide variation from teenagers to late 60s 
and is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. Muscle biopsy 
shows myofibrillar dissolution.43
Emery-Dreifuss Muscular Dystrophy.  EDMD is a unique  
inherited muscular dystrophy characterized by muscle weakness, 
joint contractures, and cardiac dysfunction. Muscle weakness 
commonly presents in a humeroperoneal pattern, preferentially 
affecting the biceps and triceps in the arms and the ankle dorsi -
flexors and evertors in the legs. In contrast to most myopathies, 
the shoulders, hips, and thighs are usually spared. Neck weakness 
and scapular winging are also commonly seen. Contractures fre -
quently present by the first decade of life and classically affect 
neck extension, elbow flexion, and ankle plantarflexion. The  
majority of EDMD patients have cardiac dysfunction, which can 
range from arrhythmias, syncope, or exercise intolerance to car -
diomyopathy and heart failure.21
Diagnostic Evaluation. CK levels can range from normal to  
15 times the upper limit of normal. Muscle biopsy shows dystro -
phic features, disruption of myofibrillar architecture, and lack of 
antiemerin antibodies. Electrodiagnostic studies show EDMD 
results from defect of one or more proteins in the nuclear enve -
lope, possibly caused by dysfunction of protein importation into 
the nucleus. Many genes can cause this phenotype, including 
EMD, LMNA, SYNE1, SYNE2, SUN1, and SUN2.
Management. Physical therapy for flexibility and range of  
motion (ROM) should be initiated for contractures; however, 
often these contractures are severe and require surgical interven -
tion. Achilles tendon–lengthening procedures have shown the 
most efficacy. Elbow flexion contracture surgery can be more 
complicated and has a lower rate of success. Surgical intervention 
for neck contractures can be considered, although the risks and 
benefits must be carefully weighed.
Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy. FSHD is the third 
most common form of muscular dystrophy, resulting from abnor-
mal expression of the DUX4 gene in skeletal muscle. The two 
subtypes of FSHD have similar clinical presentations and molecu-
lar basis but distinct genotypes. FSHD-1 accounts for 95% of 
cases, and FSHD-2 makes up the remaining 5%. 49 It typically 
presents in the second decade of life, though it may present as 
early as infancy. It is autosomal dominant, though up to 30% of 
cases arise from sporadic mutations. FSHD is characterized by 
facial weakness, scapular winging, and humeral muscle weakness 
with regional sparing of the deltoids. The pattern of weakness of 
FSHD may notably be asymmetric. Extramuscular manifestations 
are less pronounced than in other types of muscular dystrophy 
and more common in childhood presentations. These manifesta -
tions may include respiratory involvement, retinal vascular dis -
ease, hearing loss, and possible cardiac abnormalities.
Diagnostic Evaluation.  In patients with a classic phenotype 
and genetically confirmed FSHD in a first-degree relative, a 
clinical diagnosis is often sufficient. Genetic testing is the defini -
tive diagnostic modality for identifying FSHD. Most protocols 
start by analyzing the size of D4Z4 repeat array. FSHD-1 results 
from a D4Z4 repeat contraction and therefore cannot be identi -
fied on whole exome sequencing. If no D4Z4 repeat contraction 
is identified, further testing for FSHD-2 is performed by assess -
ing the degree of methylation of the 4q35 subtelomeric region. 34 
Serum CK levels may be normal or elevated, but not more than 
five times the upper limit of normal. Electrodiagnostic testing 
and muscle biopsy often demonstrate nonspecific myopathic 
features.
Management. There is currently no validated pharmacologic 
treatment to treat or slow the progression of FSHD. Engaging in 
low-intensity aerobic exercise with an experienced physical thera-
pist may be beneficial, although it is important to not overexert 
the patient to the point of worsening muscle weakness. While 
respiratory compromise is less common in FSHD, baseline PFTs 
should be obtained at the time of diagnosis. Those with abnormal 
PFTs and/or excessive daytime sleepiness (EDS) should have  
interval monitoring and a referral for a sleep study, as some  
patients may require nocturnal NIV . Routine cardiac monitoring 
is not indicated, though any patient with cardiac signs or symp -
toms should undergo further testing. Those with large D4Z4  
repeat contractions may be at risk of retinal vascular disease and

Oculopharyngeal Muscular Dystrophy. OPMD is a late-onset 
myopathy characterized by weakness of the eyelid and bulbar 
muscles. Patients present in the fifth or sixth decade of life with 
progressive ptosis and dysphagia. In later stages of disease, extra -
ocular movements can be affected leading to diplopia, and there 
can be mild weakness in the facial, temporal, and masseter 
muscles. Patients can also have proximal weakness in the shoul -
ders or pelvic girdle. Intrinsic eye muscles and retinal function 
are preserved. OPMD is caused by GCG expansions of the 
polyadenylate-binding protein nuclear-1 ( PABPN1) gene. This 
disorder usually has an autosomal dominant pattern of inheri -
tance, although there is a rarer autosomal recessive form.
Diagnostic Evaluation. Genetic testing for the expansions in 
the PABPN1 gene is the primary diagnostic tool used for sus -
pected OPMD. Laboratory testing can show an elevated CK. 
Muscle biopsy shows tubulofilamentous intranuclear inclusions in 
the skeletal muscle. Needle EMG of weak muscles demonstrates 
a myopathic pattern.6
Management. Dysphagia can be evaluated by speech therapy 
and objectively measured with modified barium swallow test  
or videofluoroscopic swallowing study (VFSS). Patients should 
undergo diet modification to the least restrictive diet that can be 
safely tolerated. Severe weight loss, malnutrition, and aspiration 
pneumonia are possible complications of untreated dysphagia. 
Blepharoplasty or frontal suspension of eyelids can be considered 
for ptosis that is impairing vision.
Distal Myopathies
Distal inherited myopathies present with weakness and atrophy of 
the distal legs and hands. Symptom onset is variable, but com -
pared to other inherited myopathies, there is more onset likely 
toward late adulthood. On physical exam, distal myopathies often 
have preferential weakness of the ankle dorsiflexors compared to 
the toe extension. This is in contrast to a neuropathic pattern of 
weakness in which the distal toe extensors are weaker than the 
dorsiflexors and can be a useful clinical tool in diagnosing distal 
myopathies.
Welander Distal Myopathy. Welander myopathy is an autoso-
mal dominant late-onset disease with symptoms occurring in the 
fifth and sixth decades of life. This disease is most often found in 
the Swedish and Finnish populations. Classically, weakness is 
found in the long finger extensors and ankle dorsiflexion. There 
may be mild sensory dysfunction as well. The gene TIA1 is  
involved. There is almost no cardiac or pulmonary involvement.10
Markesbery-Griggs/Udd Late-Onset Distal Myopathy.  
Markesbery-Griggs distal myopathy typically presents with weak-
ness in the anterior leg compartment in muscles in the fourth to 
fifth decades of life. Later in the disease course, the distal fingers and 
proximal arms and legs may be affected as well. Genetic testing 
shows mutation in the TTN gene.10
Nonaka Distal Myopathy. Nonaka distal myopathy is an auto-
somal recessive disorder with disease onset in the 20s. Weakness is 
found preferentially in dorsiflexion and toe extension. Cardiac 
conduction abnormalities may be present. Mutations in the GNE 
gene are causative.10
Myotonic Dystrophy
The myotonic muscular dystrophies are multisystem diseases 
characterized by autosomal dominant repeat expansions. There 
are two types of myotonic dystrophy. Myotonic dystrophy type 1 
(DM1) results from a CTG triplet expansion in the DMPK gene, 
whereas myotonic dystrophy 2 (DM2) results from a CCTG tet -
ramer expansion in the CNBP gene.19 As the name suggests, the 
myotonic dystrophies are characterized by myotonia. Though 
patients may describe symptoms that sound similar to muscle 
cramps, myotonia has distinct pathophysiology and electrodiag -
nostic features. Myotonia originates at the level of the muscle  
fiber, whereas muscle cramps are generated at the level of the  
motor neuron/axon. During needle EMG, myotonic discharges 
are recognized as spontaneous discharges firing at a rate of 20 to 
150 Hz with waxing and waning amplitude and frequency. Myo-
tonic discharges have brief spike or positive sharp wave morphol -
ogy39 (Video 43.3). Clinically, patients may experience myotonia 
as delayed muscle relaxation. Patients with grip myotonia, for 
example, may express difficulty releasing their grasp on a door -
knob or after a firm handshake. Myotonia is also exacerbated by 
cold temperatures.
Myotonic Dystrophy 1.  Patients with DM1 have a higher 
number of CTG repeats ( .50) than those without DM1. Early 
studies of DM1 suggested that a higher number of repeats cor -
related with greater disease severity and earlier onset, though 
recent data suggest that other factors, such as downstream  
sequence interruption and concomitant mutations in other 
genes, also influence severity. The most severe form of DM1 is 
congenital, where symptom onset occurs within the first few 
months of life. Congenital DM1 is often (but not always) char -
acterized by an exceptionally high number of CTG repeats 
(.750). This occurs most often because of the phenomenon of 
anticipation, where intergenerational expansion leads to repeat 
instability and a more severe phenotype. Infants with congenital 
DM1 may experience hypotonia, respiratory failure, skeletal 
deformities, and impaired feeding. It is not uncommon for new -
borns with congenital DM1 to require ventilatory and nutri -
tional support. Those with disease onset between 4 weeks and 
10 years of life fall into the category of childhood-onset DM1, 
and those with onset between 10 and 18 years of life fall into the 
category of juvenile-onset DM1. 19 The remainder of patients 
will develop symptoms in adulthood. DM1 preferentially affects 
muscles of the face, neck/pharynx, trunk, and distal limbs.  
Facial appearance may appear thin with temporal wasting, and 
ptosis is common. Patients may experience dysarthria, dyspha -
gia, and difficulty lifting their head off a pillow caused by neck 
flexor weakness. Proximal muscles may be involved later in the 
course. Extramuscular manifestations of DM1 include endo -
crine/metabolic abnormalities, cardiac arrhythmias, cognitive 
impairment, apathy, EDS, central sleep apnea, impaired gastro -
intestinal motility, hypersensitivity to anesthetics, increased risk 
of cancer, and cataracts.
Myotonic Dystrophy 2. DM2 often presents later in life than 
DM1, with symptom onset typically in the third to fifth decade.39 
The average number of CCTG repeats in DM2 is 5000, and there 
have been no congenital or infantile cases reported. The variability 
in disease severity in DM2 is not clearly understood. Repeat length 
in blood cells has not been shown to correlate with disease sever -
ity.19 Patients with DM2 often develop proximal rather than distal 
muscle weakness. Patients may describe myalgia, stiffness with ini-
tiating activity, or difficulty ascending stairs. It is not uncommon 
for patients with DM2 to go years without the correct diagnosis, 
which is likely because of milder symptoms, later onset, slower 
progression, and less specificity in presentation. Despite a milder 
phenotype than DM1 in general, patients can suffer many of the 
same extramuscular complications later in the disease course.

Diagnosis. Genetic testing is the diagnostic modality of choice 
to confirm myotonic dystrophy. In those with typical clinical 
features and a suspected or genetically confirmed family history, 
proceeding directly to genetic testing is appropriate. In those with 
less specific clinical presentation, labs and/or EMG may be per -
formed before genetic testing. CK level may be normal or mini -
mally elevated. EMG is characterized by myotonic discharges and 
motor units with a myopathic morphology and recruitment pat -
tern in the typical distribution depending on subtype. Muscle 
pathology often shows nonspecific myopathic features, and it is 
not required to make the diagnosis.
Management. The consensus care guidelines developed by the 
Muscular Dystrophy Foundation provide similar guidance in both 
types of myotonic dystrophy.2,42 Patients with myotonic dystrophy 
can have severe adverse reactions to anesthetics and opioid medica-
tions; therefore anesthesia risks should be discussed before surgical 
or procedural intervention. As pulmonary complications are the 
leading cause of death in DM1, baseline and annual PFTs should 
be conducted. Patients with respiratory involvement and/or EDS 
should be referred to pulmonology. NIV is often recommended for 
patients with more significant respiratory impairment. Patients 
with EDS may be counseled about lifestyle interventions and/or 
prescribed medications to help with alertness, such as modafinil. 
Cardiac complications are the second leading cause of death in 
DM1, and ECGs should be obtained annually or biennially. Those 
with abnormal ECGs or cardiac symptoms should be referred to 
cardiology. Patients interested in family planning should undergo 
genetic counseling to understand the risks of inheritance in off -
spring. Patients planning to become pregnant should also be coun-
seled about the increased risk of complications and be referred  
to a high-risk obstetrician. Speech, swallow, mobility, fall risk,  
and independence with ADLs should be evaluated annually with  
appropriate referrals to respective therapists when indicated.  
Mexiletine may be considered to manage discomfort or dysfunc -
tion secondary to myotonia, though this is contraindicated in  
patients with cardiac arrythmias. Patients should undergo annual 
eye exams to screen for ocular manifestations of myotonic dystro-
phy, such as cataracts. Patients should be screened for gastrointes-
tinal involvement with appropriate referral to a specialist when 
indicated. Patients with psychiatric or behavioral manifestations 
should be referred to an appropriate mental health provider. For 
metabolic/endocrine manifestations, hemoglobin A1c and liver 
function tests should be obtained annually. Thyroid function tests 
and lipid panels should be obtained every 3 years. Sex-specific 
endocrine abnormalities, such as erectile dysfunction or irregular 
menstruation, warrant referral to appropriate specialists. Finally, 
patients with myotonic dystrophy should be counseled about the 
increased risk of cancer and follow general population cancer 
screening guidelines.2
Channelopathies: Nondystrophic Myotonias and Periodic 
Paralyses. Channelopathies are a group of inherited diseases 
caused by mutations in electrolyte channels (sodium, potassium, 
and chloride) in the muscle membrane. In the nondystrophic 
myotonias, such as myotonia congenita (mutations in chloride 
channel protein CLCN1), this can present with muscle stiffness 
and delayed relaxation after contraction. Clinically, this can be 
evaluated by having the patient grip tightly for several seconds 
and assess for delayed relaxation. Sometimes symptoms improve 
with repeated muscle contractions, in a “warmup” phenomenon. 
Usually there is no fixed weakness.
Periodic paralyses disorders are characterized by episodes of 
flaccid muscle paralysis without loss of consciousness. Symptoms 
typically start before the age of 20 years. Attacks of episodic  
paralysis can last from minutes to hours. Mutations in the sodium 
channel gene SCN4A and calcium channel gene CACNA1S are 
associated with periodic paralysis. T riggers can involve tempera -
ture, certain carbohydrate-rich foods, stress, and changes in the 
level of extracellular potassium. 45 Depolarizing anesthetic agents 
should be avoided in patients with any channelopathy.
Congenital Myopathies
The congenital myopathies are a group of heterogeneous disorders 
historically grouped by specific histopathologic features on muscle 
biopsy. Included in this group are the nemaline myopathies, core 
myopathies, and centronuclear myopathies. They are character -
ized by pathology at the level of the contractile matrix, involving 
structures such as T tubules or the sarcoplasmic reticulum. More 
recently with advances in genetic testing, several genes of interest 
have been identified. There is significant variability in time of 
onset and phenotypic features across the congenital myopathies. 
Symptoms may include hypotonia, joint contractures and other 
musculoskeletal deformities, ophthalmoparesis, ptosis, and car -
diac involvement, among other manifestations. Patients with 
features concerning for a congenital myopathy require prompt 
referral to a pediatric neuromuscular specialist to pursue genetic 
testing. This often avoids the need for costly or invasive diagnostic 
studies, such as EMG, muscle biopsy, or advanced imaging. As 
with many of the myopathic diseases discussed in this chapter, no 
specific medications exist to treat these diseases, and caring for 
these patients requires a multidisciplinary approach focused  
on supportive care and optimization of function. 7 Table 43.3 
shows the common congenital myopathic disorders and their 
characteristics.
Metabolic Myopathies
Metabolic myopathies are a group of inherited neuromuscular 
disorders caused by deficiency of enzymes necessary for carbohy -
drate or lipid metabolism, leading to exercise intolerance, rhabdo-
myolysis, and sometimes multiorgan involvement. During short 
bouts of high-intensity exercise, the primary metabolic pathways 
involve the breakdown of glycogen or anaerobic breakdown of 
glucose. Thus disorders of carbohydrate metabolism are present 
within this early phase of exercise. Conversely, with prolonged 
exercise, the body begins to rely on free fatty acid metabolism. 
Lipid metabolic disorders present in situations of low-intensity, 
long-duration exercise. 3 Mitochondrial myopathies can present 
similarly to lipid myopathies.
Diagnosis
Elevation of creatinine kinase and aldolase levels after physical 
activity are often the first signs of metabolic myopathies. Recent 
advances and widespread availability of molecular genetic testing 
have greatly expedited the diagnosis of these disorders. There are 
commercially available panels that test dozens of genes coding for 
metabolic enzymes simultaneously from a simple saliva sample. 
Often the diagnosis can be made from genetic testing alone. 
However, difficulties arise when there are VUS, in which there is 
a detected mutation in a causative gene; however, current technol-
ogy cannot determine whether this mutation would be disruptive 
to normal protein function. In cases of VUS, muscle biopsies, 
electrodiagnostic testing, forearm testing, bloodwork for glucose 
and lipid metabolites, and cycle exercise testing may be indicated.

Disorders of Glycogen Metabolism
Patients with GSD develop exercise intolerance and cramps 
within minutes of exercise. McArdle disease (GSD-V) is the most 
common metabolic myopathy and caused by a mutation in the 
PYGM gene encoding for myophosphorylase. McArdle disease 
and the majority of GSD are inherited via an autosomal recessive 
pattern. Individuals with McArdle disease experience a “second 
wind” phenomenon, where after a period of low-intensity exercise 
there is then a decrease in heart rate and an improvement in exer-
cise tolerance. This is caused by the initiation of fatty acid  
metabolism. Prolonged exertion results in profound muscle stiff -
ness, temporary muscle contractures, rhabdomyolysis, and renal 
failure. Many individuals with McArdle disease are diagnosed  
after repeated bouts of elevated creatinine kinase levels leading to 
hospitalization. With disease progression, about 50% of McArdle 
disease patients have fixed weakness of the proximal and axial 
muscles. Many cases of McArdle disease are diagnosed through 
genetic testing. The exercise forearm test should show a normal 
rise in serum ammonia without significant rise in lactic acid. 
Muscle biopsy shows absent staining for myophosphorylase and 
accumulation of glycogen in vacuoles.
Disorders of Fatty Acid Oxidation
Lipid myopathies present with exercise intolerance and myalgias 
with low-intensity, long-duration exercises. Stiffness is less common. 
Rhabdomyolysis and elevated CK can also be seen with fasting, ill-
ness, cold, or surgical procedures. CPT-II deficiency is the most 
common lipid myopathy, which can be diagnosed through genetic 
testing. Laboratory studies show increased long-chain fatty acids on 
acyl carnitine profiling.
Management
Individuals with metabolic myopathies can engage in exercise 
safely if progressed in a slow, gradual manner. Dietary modifica -
tion is key to successful therapy, and individuals with McArdle 
disease may benefit from a ketogenic diet and taking sugars pre -
exercise. For lipid myopathies, the diet should be rich in carbohy-
drates and low in fats.
3
Pompe Disease
Pompe disease is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by 
glycogen deposits within the lysosomes of muscle fibers. The caus-
ative gene is GAA, which encodes alpha-glucosidase, an enzyme 
catalyzing alpha-1,4 and alpha-1,6 linkages in lysosomal glyco -
gen. Pompe disease can present in either early-onset (infantile) or 
late-onset (adult) form, and the disease activity is related to the 
amount of residual enzyme activity in the muscle cells. Diagnosis 
is made by genetic testing or decreased enzyme activity in dried 
blood spot testing. Myotonic discharges can be seen in the para -
spinal muscles on needle EMG. Early-onset Pompe disease typi -
cally presents before the age of 1 year and is characterized by

Mitochondrial Myopathies
Mitochondrial myopathies are a large group of disorders defined 
by genetic abnormalities resulting in pathologic changes to the 
structure or function of mitochondria. Mitochondrial dysfunc -
tion can negatively impact essentially any organ system, and the 
nervous and muscular systems are often involved because of their 
high energy requirements. The specific subtypes of mitochon -
drial diseases are often eponyms (e.g., Kearns-Sayre syndrome) or 
acronyms derived from the subtype’s common manifestations 
(e.g., MELAS [mitochondrial encephalopathy, lactic acidosis, 
and strokelike syndrome]). In general, the current names that 
exist to describe mitochondrial diseases do not effectively classify 
the incredible range of genotypic and phenotypic presentations 
associated with these disorders. T o demonstrate this range in 
clinical presentation, a few of the major phenotypes are briefly 
discussed here. Leber hereditary optic neuropathy is character -
ized by subacute painless vision loss. MELAS is characterized  
by myopathy, seizure disorders, short stature, cognitive impair -
ments, and strokelike events in nonvascular territories. Myo -
clonic epilepsy with ragged red fibers is characterized by epilepsy, 
short stature, sensorineural hearing loss, cardiac rhythm abnor -
malities, myopathy, and characteristic ragged red fibers on mus -
cle biopsy. Leigh syndrome and neuropathy, ataxia, and retinitis 
pigmentosa (or NARP) is characterized by a stepwise decline in 
neurologic function, peripheral neuropathy, retinitis pigmentosa, 
ventilatory failure, and characteristic findings on MRI brain 
showing involvement of central gray matter, cerebellar nuclei, 
periaqueductal gray, and brainstem. The mtDNA deletion and 
depletion syndromes, such as progressive external ophthalmople -
gia, result in a large range of hepatocerebral, encephalomyop -
athic, or neurogastrointestinal manifestations. Kearns-Sayre  
syndrome results in external ophthalmoplegia, retinitis pigmen -
tosa, sensorineural loss, myopathy, and cardiac abnormalities. 
Pearson syndrome results in death within the first few years of life 
due sideroblastic anemia, multiple severe endocrinopathies, and 
pancreatic failure. Other mitochondrial phenotypes include the 
POLG spectrum disorders, mitochondrial neurogastrointestinal 
encephalopathy, BCS1L syndromes, OPA1 disorders, and mito -
chondrial myopathy caused by thymidine kinase-2 deficiency. 9 
The diagnostic approach requires an in-depth understanding of 
key phenotypic features; therefore involvement of an experienced 
neuromuscular specialist is critically important. Evaluation with 
blood tests, genetic testing, and muscle biopsy may aid in the 
diagnosis. Unfortunately, there are currently no FDA-approved 
medications or therapies for mitochondrial diseases, and the evi -
dence to support the use of vitamins and cofactors is lacking.
Acquired Myopathies
Idiopathic Inflammatory Myopathies
The idiopathic inflammatory myopathies (IIMs) are a group of 
multisystem diseases associated with myositis. These disorders 
most commonly develop between the fourth and sixth decades  
of life, though children between the ages of 5 and 15 years may 
also develop subtypes of IIMs. The majority of these subtypes 
disproportionately affect females (ratio 2:1) except for IBM. 33 
Historically, polymyositis (PM) was the umbrella term for these 
disorders. As more was learned about specific clinical features, 
histopathologic manifestations, and serologic autoantibodies, the 
term PM was eventually separated into specific diseases, such as 
antisynthetase syndrome (ASyS) and immune-mediated necrotiz-
ing myopathy (IMNM). Muscle enzymes may be normal or dra -
matically elevated (.20 times the upper limit of normal). Nerve 
conduction studies are typically normal in IIMs. Needle EMG 
may show abnormal spontaneous activity (fibrillation potentials, 
positive sharp waves, complex repetitive discharges, or myotonic 
discharges) when significant inflammation and/or necrosis is pres-
ent, decreased insertional activity in advanced disease with sig -
nificant fibrosis, and myopathic motor units with short duration, 
polyphasia, low amplitude, and early recruitment. Muscle MRI is 
typically obtained to distinguish muscle edema from fat infiltra -
tion, determine the pattern and extent of muscle involvement, 
assist in muscle selection for biopsy, and assess the response to 
therapy. Muscle biopsy remains the diagnostic tool of choice to 
definitively identify the specific subtype in IIMs. T reatment pro-
tocols vary by subtype but typically involve corticosteroids and 
nonsteroidal immunosuppressant drugs often in combination. 30 
Both rheumatologists and neuromuscular specialists are often  
involved in the care of patients with IIMs. Table 43.4 shows the 
most common inflammatory myopathic disorders and their rele -
vant characteristics.
Dermatomyositis
Dermatomyositis is characterized by proximal muscle weakness 
and typical dermatologic signs, such as Gottron papules, me -
chanic’s hands, and heliotrope rash. Patients may also experience 
constitutional symptoms, calcinosis cutis, cardiac involvement, 
and interstitial lung disease, among other manifestations. Der -
matomyositis can be classified into further subtypes based on 
specific autoantibodies, each showcasing distinct features in 
terms of muscle weakness distribution and extramuscular symp -
toms. Common autoantibodies associated with dermatomyositis 
include anti-Mi-2, anti-MDA5, anti-TIF1-gamma, anti-NXP2, 
and anti-SAE.28 In some cases, an autoantibody cannot be iden -
tified. Dermatomyositis is strongly associated with malignancy, 
especially among patients with anti-TIF1-gamma and anti-
NXP2 antibodies. 14 Muscle MRI often shows patchy muscle 
edema. Muscle biopsy classically demonstrates perifascicular  
atrophy, among other features.
Antisynthetase Syndrome
The clinical features of ASyS include myositis, fever, rash, intersti-
tial lung disease, arthritis, mechanic’s hands, and Raynaud syn -
drome. There are eight known antibodies associated with ASyS: 
anti-Jo1, anti-Ha/YRS, anti-Zo, anti-EJ, anti-PL-7, anti-PL-12, 
anti-OJ, and anti-KS.28
Immune-Mediated Necrotizing Myopathy
IMNM is most commonly associated with anti–signal recognition 
particle (anti-SRP) or anti-3–HMG-CoA reductase. Anti-SRP 
antibodies are associated with a severe and rapid-onset presenta -
tion of weakness and CK levels over 10,000. Muscle MRI often 
shows disproportionate involvement of pelvic and hip adductor 
muscles. Myocarditis and respiratory failure may develop in anti-
SRP IMNM, which often requires aggressive treatment. IMNM 
associated with anti-HMG-CoA reductase antibodies may present

Inclusion Body Myositis
IBM is the most common acquired muscle disease affecting 
individuals in the second half of life. The classic phenotype 
involves atrophy of the deep forearm flexors and quadriceps.  
As the disease advances, often all skeletal muscles become  
involved. Aspiration pneumonia secondary to dysphagia is a 
leading cause of mortality in IBM. It is often misdiagnosed as 
a different IIM, which can delay the diagnosis by several years. 
Some patients may have positive anti-cytosolic 5´-nucleotidase 
1A antibodies, although studies have shown that this antibody 
is also present in other neuromuscular diseases. EMG often 
shows a combination of neuropathic and myopathic features. 
Key features on histopathology include the invasion of nonne -
crotic myofibers by lymphocytes, rimmed vacuoles, and major 
histocompatibility complex I and II overexpression. Unlike the 
aforementioned IIMs, IBM does not improve with immuno -
suppressive therapies.
16
Endocrine Myopathies
Low vitamin D levels (, 30 nmol/L) can lead to elevated serum 
CK levels, exercise intolerance, and rhabdomyolysis. Both hypo- 
and hyperthyroidism can predispose patients to exertional rhabdo-
myolysis. Hypothyroidism has also been associated with proximal 
myopathies. In fact, up to 80% of patients with hypothyroidism 
report some degree of muscle cramping, muscle fatigue, or weak-
ness.
13 Myopathy secondary to corticosteroids can occur because 
of both exogenous (prescription medications) and endogenous 
(adrenal tumors, ectopic adrenocorticotropic hormone produc-
tion, and pituitary adenomas) sources. Patients typically have other 
features of Cushing syndrome in addition to muscle weakness, 
including hirsutism, impaired glucose metabolism, central obesity, 
purple striae, and neuropsychiatric features, among other signs. 
Excessive growth hormone (acromegaly) is also known to lead to a 
progressive proximal myopathy. Though significantly less com-
mon, it should be noted that diabetes, gonadal dysfunction, and 
carcinoid syndrome can rarely lead to myonecrosis, muscle fatigue, 
or myopathy.
24
Toxic Myopathies
The clinical manifestation of toxic myopathy varies widely.  
Although some patients experience severe symptoms shortly  
after starting the medication responsible, others may develop 
mild symptoms gradually over several months of exposure. 
Cholesterol-lowering medications, cyclosporine, propofol,  
labetalol, and alcohol can all lead to necrotizing myopathies. 
Presentation may include proximal muscle weakness, hyperCK -
emia, an irritable myopathy on EMG, and occasionally renal 
involvement. Patients often recover once the offending agent has 
been identified and the patient is no longer exposed. It is impor -
tant to note that statins can lead to either a toxic necrotizing 
myopathy or IMNM (see earlier). Patients diagnosed with the 
latter will not recover with medication discontinuation alone,

Immune checkpoint inhibitors, D-penicillamine, cimetidine,
