the disruption of lysosomal function. Colchicine and vincristine 
may lead to antimicrotubular myopathies through the disruption 
of microtubules.
Recreational drugs can lead to various muscle pathologies.  
Repeated intramuscular injection of drugs can lead to focal muscle 
fibrosis. Individuals who use cocaine, phencyclidine, or amphet -
amines may experience rhabdomyolysis. Rhabdomyolysis may also 
occur with compartment syndrome secondary to immobility  
related to drug use.11
Infectious Myopathies
Infection by bacteria, viruses, fungi, or parasites is a less common 
etiology of muscle disease, though it is critically important to 
recognize. The presence of fever, malaise, leukocytosis, and ele -
vated inflammatory markers should prompt consideration of an 
infectious source. A clinical history of spending time in tropical 
regions, recent infection elsewhere in the body, penetrating inju -
ries, recent surgery, intramuscular drug use, immunosuppression, 
muscle ischemia, or exposure to unsanitary conditions may all 
raise suspicion for infectious myositis. A myriad of pathogens are 
capable of infecting muscle. Viruses and parasites often lead to 
diffuse myalgia or multifocal myositis, while bacteria tend to 
cause focal involvement. Diagnosis relies on laboratory investiga -
tions, cultures and/or muscle biopsies, and imaging. Consultation 
with an infectious disease specialist may be indicated. T reatment 
with appropriate antimicrobials can prevent severe complications, 
such as sepsis.35
Critical Illness Myopathy
CIM is a myopathy that can develop in severely ill, hospitalized, 
or intensive care unit (ICU) patients. CIM can occur with or 
without critical illness polyneuropathy. CIM can be a significant 
factor in delayed recovery and prolonged mechanical ventilation. 
Risk factors for CIM include prolonged ICU stay, mechanical 
ventilation, severity of acute disease course, sepsis, multiorgan 
failure, parenteral nutrition, and administration of corticosteroids 
and aminoglycosides. 54,56 The underlying mechanisms for CIM 
are poorly understood but may involve abnormal depolarization 
of muscle membrane from systemic insult.
The diagnosis of CIM includes (1) a positive history for 
critical illness with multiorgan dysfunction, (2) clinical limb 
weakness or difficulty in weaning patient from ventilation, 
and (3) exclusion of other medical and neurologic causes for 
increased weakness. Electrodiagnostic testing shows small-
amplitude, short-duration motor units with early recruitment 
with or without fibrillation potentials and positive sharp 
waves. Muscle biopsy shows myopathy with necrosis. Aggres -
sive treatment of sepsis and decreasing doses of corticosteroids 
and neuromuscular blocking agents may decrease likelihood 
of CIM. Early mobilization with physical therapy is recom -
mended when appropriate in ICU patients.
General Rehabilitation Principles in 
Myopathic Disorders
Despite the incredible range of clinical severity and pathology of 
the disorders discussed in this chapter, there are several common 
rehabilitation principles that can be applied when implementing 
a care plan for patients with muscle disease. As is the case with 
other chronic conditions managed by physiatrists, the approach 
to care often involves an interdisciplinary model. It is common  
for care teams to include physical therapists, occupational thera -
pists, speech therapists, rehabilitation psychologists, nutritionists,  
social workers, assistive technology representatives, orthotists, and 
wheelchair vendors. Representatives from volunteer organiza -
tions, such as the Muscular Dystrophy Association, may also be 
present in interdisciplinary clinics. Physician team members may 
include physiatrists, neurologists, rheumatologists, orthopedic 
surgeons, endocrinologists, pulmonologists, and cardiologists. 
Principles of care are further discussed by topic.
Mobility and Exercise
A strength and mobility assessment should take place at each visit, 
ideally in conjunction with a physical therapist. The distribution 
and severity of weakness are highly variable across muscle diseases; 
therefore some patients may require a single-point cane for assis -
tance in ambulation, while others may need a power wheelchair 
for all mobility. T ransfer boards, transfer benches, and lifts facili-
tate safe and comfortable transfers between different surfaces (e.g., 
bed to wheelchair, wheelchair to toilet) for individuals with 
muscle weakness or limited mobility. Physical therapy can help 
recommend the appropriate mobility aids and collaborate with 
prosthetics when bracing (such as an ankle-foot orthosis) is  
recommended.
Overall, there is sparse literature about how to best provide 
guidance on exercise in patients with muscle disease. However, 
some care guidelines around exercise do exist for specific disorders. 
For example, the American Academy of Neurology’s evidence-
based guidelines for care in FSHD report that low-intensity exer-
cise appears to be safe and beneficial for these patients. 49 When 
specific guidelines do not exist, an exercise program may still be 
considered with input from other members of the care team.  
A physical therapist may be able to help develop an individualized 
exercise program for the patient while considering important fac-
tors such as the patient’s fall risk. Eccentric exercises can exacerbate 
muscle damage and are not recommended in diseases such as 
DMD. In general, exercise that results in delayed-onset muscle 
soreness is not recommended by most neuromuscular physicians. 
When active movement at a joint is significantly decreased or no 
longer possible, passive ROM should be employed regularly to 
avoid contracture development.4,5
Contractures
Contractures can lead to further impairments in function, skin 
breakdown, poor posture or positioning when seated, pain or 
discomfort, and difficulty reaching certain body regions for  
hygiene. ROM throughout the extremities should be regularly 
assessed both in the doctor’s office and by the patient’s caregiver(s) 
at home. Because contractures are quite difficult to treat, it is best 
to do what is possible to prevent their development to begin with. 
Frequent passive ROM exercises, stretching, and bracing can all 
help prevent contractures from developing. Patients may benefit

drinks liquids mixed with barium contrast to evaluate the safety 
and efficiency of swallowing and the bolus movement. Speech 
therapy often relies on these study results to make recommenda -
tions about oral intake, such as thickening liquids. In some cases, 
study results will show that the patient is at a high enough risk of 
aspiration that it is not recommended to take any nutrition by 
mouth. In these cases, a risk-benefit discussion with the patient 
about taking food and liquids by mouth vs. enteral nutrition 
should take place. Patients with dysphagia should also be screened 
for respiratory dysfunction because these symptoms are often 
present together. Severely impaired respiratory function may pre-
clude patients from undergoing procedures for enteral tube place-
ment; therefore these discussions should occur early in the disease 
course if possible. Sialorrhea can often be managed with anticho -
linergic medications such as glycopyrrolate, though patients may 
begin to experience the opposite problem of dry mouth. Injecting 
botulinum toxin into the salivary glands can significantly reduce 
saliva production, and this may be considered on a case-by-case 
basis depending on the patient’s specific disease and comorbidities 
(see Chapter 3).
Nutrition
Dietitians are important team members in providing comprehen-
sive care for patients with muscle disease. During their initial assess-
ment, they will obtain information regarding the patient’s current 
nutritional status, dietary intake, and body composition. Depend-
ing on the specific disorder, some patients may need assistance with 
gaining weight while others (such as DMD patients on steroids) 
may need assistance in avoiding weight gain. Guidance from a  
dietitian is especially important for those with enteral feeding to 
ensure the patient is receiving enough essential nutrients.
Cognitive Impairment and  
Neuropsychiatric Manifestations
Several of the disorders discussed in this chapter are associated 
with cognitive impairment or psychiatric comorbidities. For 
example, DM1 is well known to be associated with impaired 
cognition and apathetic or anxious personality traits. 8 Atten -
tion-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, autism spectrum disorders, 
anxiety, depression, and obsessive-compulsive disorder are more 
common in patients with DMD and BMD than in the general 
population.38 It is important to address mood and cognitive 
impairment in the patient’s history periodically to identify  
affected patients. Talking with family members or caregivers  
(if permitted by the patient) can also be helpful in identifying 
behaviors or impairments that the patient may be unaware of. 
Depending on the patient’s presentation, referrals to psychiatry, 
speech therapy, neuropsychiatric testing, and/or psychology may 
be useful.
Respiratory Care
Neuromuscular weakness leading to restrictive lung disease is one 
of the most serious manifestations in myopathic disorders. It is 
important to work closely with a respiratory therapist and obtain 
routine PFTs in those at risk for respiratory involvement. A refer-
ral to pulmonology is also warranted early in the course. Patients 
often require NIV , such as bilevel positive airway pressure, later in 
the disease course (Fig. 43.9).

If contractures have already developed, management options  
depend on the affected body region, the patient’s age, and the 
patient’s individual situation. Surgical interventions for address -
ing contractures are often better suited to the pediatric popula -
tion, though certain adults may also benefit from a referral to 
orthopedic surgery to discuss options.
Activities of Daily Living and Equipment
Outside of mobility aids, patients with muscle disease often  
require prescriptions for several other kinds of equipment.  
Understanding which equipment is needed comes from inquir -
ing about the level of assistance the patient requires to complete 
ADLs. Collaborating with occupational therapy and/or an assis -
tive technology specialist to identify tools to maximize a patient’s 
independence is helpful. Upper extremity splints, adaptive uten -
sils, universal cuffs, voice-activated technology, and forearm  
orthoses are just some examples of tools that can be used to  
optimize a patient’s function.
Specialized seating systems, cushions, and positioning aids 
help individuals with muscle disease maintain proper posture, 
alignment, and comfort while sitting or lying down. Grab bars, 
shower chairs, raised toilet seats, and other bathroom safety  
aids improve accessibility and safety in the bathroom for indi -
viduals with muscle weakness or balance issues. Speech-generating  
devices, communication boards, and adaptive keyboards can assist 
individuals with muscle weakness affecting speech or fine motor 
skills in communicating effectively. Home adaptations such as 
ramps, stairlifts, widened doorways, and grab bars may be neces -
sary to improve accessibility and safety for individuals with mus -
cle disease who have mobility limitations.
Pain Management
Pain is highly prevalent in individuals with muscle diseases. One 
study of DMD patients stated that 73% of study participants  
reported pain. There are often multiple contributors to pain in 
muscle disease, and a comprehensive history and physical exam 
are required to differentiate possible etiologies. Patients with dis -
eases such as myotonic dystrophy may describe painful cramps, 
which can be treated with medication (e.g., mexiletine). More 
often, however, pain in muscle diseases often stems from muscu -
loskeletal etiologies. Progressive weakness can lead to altered bio -
mechanics and posture, which may eventually put significant 
strain on joints, ligaments, tendons, or muscles. Impaired mobil -
ity can also lead to joint contractures, which may cause discom -
fort and stiffness. After a thorough history and examination,  
imaging with ultrasound, radiographs, or MRI may be useful to 
further assess the patient’s regional complaint. Bone pain should 
raise concern for pathologic fractures in patients with osteopenia 
or osteoporosis. T reatment may include bracing, therapies, non -
steroidal antiinflammatories, seating adjustments, or procedural 
intervention when indicated.37
Dysphagia and Secretion Management
Oropharyngeal dysfunction can occur in various types of muscle 
diseases. Patients may experience coughing or choking on food, 
frank aspiration events, or sialorrhea. Patients at risk for swallow-
ing disorders should be asked about these symptoms at each fol -
low-up visit. Those with a positive history should be referred to a

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
Summary
It is an exciting time for providers who treat myopathic disorders. 
Advances in genetic testing have enhanced diagnostic accuracy 
and improved our understanding of the specific pathogenic 
mechanisms involved in myopathic diseases. This has led to  
an increase in clinical trials and the development of novel thera -
peutics to slow disease progression and improve quality of life. 
Multidisciplinary rehabilitation care remains paramount, espe -
cially as patients who have traditionally pediatric diseases grow 
and transition into adult care. The physiatrist is uniquely equipped 
to provide comprehensive care for this patient population in this 
new and exciting landscape.
